{{ill|Nishiyamazuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}} is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped burial mound) located in {{ill|Kayō Town|ja|萱生町}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): * Diameter: 65 meters * Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): * Width: 70 meters * Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Tashiraka no Himemiko|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Kyōbushō|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonomiya Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kk]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}

*{{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}}

*{{ill|Higashitonotsuka Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳}}

Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): * Diameter: 65 meters * Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): * Width: 70 meters * Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kk]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}

{{ill|Nishiyamazuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}} is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped burial mound) located in {{ill|Kayō Town|ja|萱生町}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): * Diameter: 65 meters * Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): * Width: 70 meters * Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}
*{{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Nishiyamatsuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Hie Kofun|ja|ヒエ塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Higashitonotsuka Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳}}
* {{ill|Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|大和古墳群}} * [[Makimuku ruins]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] {{ill|Category:Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|Category:大和古墳群}} [[Category:Mount Miwa Kofun]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:Category]] [[ja:%E8%A5%BF%E5%B1%B1%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{ill|Nishiyamazuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}} is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped burial mound) located in {{ill|Kayō Town|ja|萱生町}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}

*{{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}}

Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}

{{ill|Nishiyamazuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}} is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped burial mound) located in {{ill|Kayō Town|ja|萱生町}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}
*{{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Nishiyamatsuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Hie Kofun|ja|ヒエ塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Higashitonotsuka Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳}}
* {{ill|Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|大和古墳群}} * [[Makimuku ruins]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] {{ill|Category:Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|Category:大和古墳群}} [[Category:Mount Miwa Kofun]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:dardar]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[ja:%E8%A5%BF%E5%B1%B1%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] {{Source Wikipedia}}

* {{ill|Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|大和古墳群}} * [[Makimuku ruins]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] {{ill|Category:Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|Category:大和古墳群}} [[Category:Mount Miwa Kofun]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[ja:%E8%A5%BF%E5%B1%B1%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.text:
{{ill|Nishiyamazuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}} is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped burial mound) located in {{ill|Kayō Town|ja|萱生町}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}
*{{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Nishiyamatsuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Hie Kofun|ja|ヒエ塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Higashitonotsuka Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳}}
* {{ill|Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|大和古墳群}} * [[Makimuku ruins]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] {{ill|Category:Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|Category:大和古墳群}} [[Category:Mount Miwa Kofun]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:Unsorted link categories]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:%E8%A5%BF%E5%B1%B1%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] {{Source Wikipedia}}

ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}
*{{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳}}

*{{ill|Higashitonotsuka Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳}}

{{ill|Nishiyamazuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}} is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped burial mound) located in {{ill|Kayō Town|ja|萱生町}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}

{{ill|Nishiyamazuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}} is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped burial mound) located in {{ill|Kayō Town|ja|萱生町}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}

* {{ill|Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|大和古墳群}} * [[Makimuku ruins]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Individual Kofun]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] {{ill|Category:Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|Category:大和古墳群}} [[Category:Mount Miwa Kofun]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:%E8%A5%BF%E5%B1%B1%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{ill|Nishiyamazuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}} is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped burial mound) located in {{ill|Kayō Town|ja|萱生町}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}
*{{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Nishiyamatsuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Hie Kofun|ja|ヒエ塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Higashitonotsuka Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳}}
* {{ill|Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|大和古墳群}} * [[Makimuku ruins]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Individual Kofun]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] {{ill|Category:Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|Category:大和古墳群}} [[Category:Mount Miwa Kofun]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[ja:%E8%A5%BF%E5%B1%B1%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] {{Source Wikipedia}}

It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tenri]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}

*{{ill|Higashitonotsuka Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳}}
* {{ill|Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|大和古墳群}} * [[Makimuku ruins]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Individual Kofun]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] {{ill|Category:Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|Category:大和古墳群}} [[Category:Mount Miwa Kofun]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:%E8%A5%BF%E5%B1%B1%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.text:
{{ill|Nishiyamazuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}} is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped burial mound) located in {{ill|Kayō Town|ja|萱生町}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:kk]][[Category::Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::History of Tenri]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}
*{{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Nishiyamatsuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Hie Kofun|ja|ヒエ塚古墳}}
*{{ill|Higashitonotsuka Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳}}
* {{ill|Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|大和古墳群}} * [[Makimuku ruins]] {{draft categories| [[Category:kk]][[Category::Individual Kofun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Zenpokoenfun]] {{ill|Category:kk]][[Category::Yamato Tumulus Group|ja|Category:kk]][[Category::大和古墳群}} [[Category:kk]][[Category::Mount Miwa Kofun]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} [[Category:kk]][[Category::Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category::Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category::pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category::Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category::pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category::Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category::pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::properly attributed pages]][[Category:kk]][[Category::Nishiyamazuka Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category::properly attributed pages]] [[ja:%E8%A5%BF%E5%B1%B1%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] {{Source Wikipedia}}

Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:kk]][[Category::Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::History of Tenri]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}

{{ill|Nishiyamazuka Kofun|ja|西山塚古墳}} is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped burial mound) located in {{ill|Kayō Town|ja|萱生町}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群}} (Kayō Subgroup) and remains unregistered as a historic site. This kofun was constructed in the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, during the early part of the late Kofun period. It is associated with a theory that identifies it as the potential burial site (真陵) of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}}, consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (the 26th Emperor of Japan). In 2024, a lodging facility called "cofunia," utilizing a traditional house on the site, opened for public use.{{Cite web |title=「古墳のふもとの古民家」を譲り受けたcofunia 前田知里さんに聞く、何もない状況から仲間を集めて宿をつくる方法 |url=https://greenz.jp/2024/09/14/cofunia/ |website=greenz.jp |access-date=2024-12-24 |language=ja |first=greenz jp |last=グリーンズ}} == Overview == The Nishiyamazuka Kofun is located at the western edge of the Kayō Town settlement, on the slopes of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}'s eastern boundary. It is a large keyhole-shaped mound.Site information board by the Tenri City Board of Education, 2010. While no excavations have been conducted to date, according to the *Yamabe District History* (山辺郡誌), artifacts were discovered during land reclamation at the summit in 1887 (Meiji 20).{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The mound faces north, with a three-tiered rear circular section and a two-tiered front rectangular section. Surface features include {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} (paving stones) and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}. A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. Artifacts such as {{ill|magatama|ja|勾玉}}, tubular beads, bells, pottery, and synthetic stones were reportedly unearthed during the Meiji-era excavation, but their current whereabouts are unknown.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} The construction date is estimated to be the early 6th century, making it one of the few late-period mounds in a region dominated by early-period kofun.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}{{Sfn|100 Kofun in Tenri|2015|p=55}} == Burial mound dimensions == [[File:西山塚古墳出土 円筒埴輪片・須恵器片.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Cylindrical haniwa fragments and Sue ware}}{{small|Exhibited at the Nara Historical and Cultural Village.}}]] The mound dimensions are as follows:Site information board. * Total length: 114 meters * Rear circular section (3-tiered): ** Diameter: 65 meters ** Height: 13 meters * Front rectangular section (2-tiered): ** Width: 70 meters ** Height: 8 meters This kofun is unique among the predominantly west-facing burial mounds of the Yamato Kofun Group, being one of only two north-facing mounds, along with the {{ill|Ninose Pond Kofun|ja|二ノ瀬池古墳}}.{{Sfn|Nishiyamazuka Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}} == Identity of the interred == [[File:821027_Imashiroduka_Kofun_aerial.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, Osaka}}{{small|Aerial view of Imashirozuka Kofun, one of Emperor Keitai's possible burial sites.}}]] While the identity of the interred remains uncertain, the mound is often associated with {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} (also called 手白髪郎女 or 手白髪命), the consort of Emperor Keitai.[[Yoshimura Takehiko]] (2010). *Series on Ancient Japanese History: Yamato Sovereignty.* Iwanami Shoten. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:kk]][[Category::Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::History of Tenri]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}

A surrounding moat is partially preserved as four reservoirs, with traces of an outer embankment visible beyond the moat. ISBN 978-4004312724.[[Shiraishi Taichiro]] (2013). *The Formation and Expansion of the Yamato State Seen from Kofun.* Keibunsha. ISBN 978-4764914324. The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} designates the burial place of Tashiraka no Himemiko as "Fusumada Tomb," situated in the Yamabe District of Yamato Province, though no precise location is detailed. In 1876, the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|ja|教部省}} assigned the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} as her official tomb, which remains the designation under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Fusumada Tomb (Kokushi)}}. However, archaeological evidence suggests that Nishitonomiya Kofun dates to the late 3rd century, inconsistent with Tashiraka no Himemiko's supposed era. The Nishiyamazuka Kofun, on the other hand, aligns more closely with her timeframe. Additional support comes from the discovery of haniwa linked to the {{ill|Shinike Haniwa Kilns|ja|新池埴輪窯跡}} in {{ill|Takatsuki|ja|高槻市}}, the same kilns associated with {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}}, believed to be Emperor Keitai’s true burial site.Taikosha (2000). *Keitai Dynasty.* p. 18. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related articles == * {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|手白香皇女}} * {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳}} * {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|ja|今城塚古墳}} [[Category:kk]][[Category::Yamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::History of Tenri]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::Yamanobe Road]] [[ja:西山塚古墳]] *{{ill|Shimoikemayama Kofun|ja|下池山古墳}}
